did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes 'DermatoPredict', a two-stage machine learning framework for automated skin disease diagnosis using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. It employs LightGBM as the primary classifier for binary cancer detection and multi-class benign disease classification, utilizing SMOTE for class balancing and feature extraction techniques.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents 'DermatoPredict', a novel two-stage machine learning framework for automated skin disease diagnosis. It details original experimental work including data preprocessing, feature extraction, model training (comparing LightGBM, SVM, etc.), and performance evaluation on the HAM10000 dataset, resulting in new accuracy metrics (81% and 85%). Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE AIEI), it reports original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: DermatoPredict: A Two-Stage Machine Learning Framework for Automated Skin Disease Diagnosis
journal: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
brief_summary: This study introduces DermatoPredict, a two-stage machine learning framework for automated skin disease diagnosis using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. The first stage classifies lesions as cancerous or non-cancerous, while the second stage further categorizes non-cancerous cases into specific benign diseases. To address class imbalance, the authors employed image preprocessing, feature flattening, and the SMOTE technique. The framework was evaluated by comparing multiple classifiers, including LightGBM, XGBoost, and SVM, with LightGBM selected as the optimal model. The proposed system achieved an accuracy of 81% in Stage 1 cancer detection and 85% in Stage 2 benign disease classification. The results demonstrate that this lightweight, interpretable approach offers a viable alternative to deep learning for early skin cancer screening in resource-limited settings.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of cancerous vs. non-cancerous lesions (Stage 1) and multi-class diagnosis of specific benign diseases (Stage 2).
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a two-stage hierarchical classification framework. Stage 1 performs binary classification to identify cancerous versus non-cancerous lesions. Stage 2 performs multi-class classification to categorize non-cancerous lesions into specific benign disease types (e.g., nevus, benign keratosis, vascular lesion, dermatofibroma). The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels using LightGBM, achieving 81% accuracy in Stage 1 and 85% accuracy in Stage 2.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A standardized and clinically valuable dataset of dermoscopic images containing identified skin lesion cases, both cancerous and non-cancerous, used as the primary data source for the DermatoPredict framework.

use.training: Used for training the two-stage machine learning framework (LightGBM) after preprocessing, flattening, and SMOTE balancing.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance via accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrices, and ROC analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To handle class imbalance in the dataset by generating synthetic samples for minority classes.
To evaluate the model's performance by visualizing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for both stages of classification.
To analyze the model's ability to distinguish between classes (malignant vs. non-cancerous) at various threshold settings and assess reliability.
To identify and rank the top features contributing to the decision-making process in the LightGBM model, enhancing interpretability.
To compare estimated odds for cancer vs. non-cancer cases and demonstrate how the model handles uncertainty and distinguishes between classes.
To show the model's confidence in its predictions for specific test cases across different disease classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrices, and ROC curves.

Stage 1 (Cancer Detection).notes: LightGBM surpassed other models (Naïve Bayes, SVM, Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, CatBoost) in accuracy, recall, and F1-score. The confusion matrix indicated good detection of non-cancerous cases and satisfactory detection of cancerous lesions. ROC curves demonstrated effective discrimination.

Stage 2 (Benign Disease Classification).notes: LightGBM consistently performed better than other implemented models in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The confusion matrix revealed high accuracy for dominating classes such as melanocytic nevus, with occasional misclassifications in visually similar and infrequent classes like vascular lesions and dermatofibroma. Prediction probability heatmaps and histograms indicated high-confidence probabilities and stable decision boundaries.

architecture_used.input_size: null (Input consists of flattened high-dimensional feature vectors derived from resized and normalized dermoscopic images)

architecture_details.stage_1: Binary classification of cancerous vs. non-cancerous lesions
architecture_details.stage_2: Multi-class classification of non-cancerous lesions into specific benign diseases (nevus, benign keratosis, vascular lesion, dermatofibroma)
architecture_details.feature_engineering: Image preprocessing (resizing, pixel normalization), label encoding, and image flattening to create numerical feature vectors compatible with tree-based classifiers

classification_or_output_head.output: Predicted class labels and probability scores

preprocessing_techniques: Image flattening to high-dimensional feature vectors
Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) for class balancing

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to address class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by generating synthetic samples for minority classes (cancerous vs. non-cancerous) to ensure equal representation during training. No specific probability or range parameters were reported.

conclusions: The proposed DermatoPredict framework, a two-stage machine learning model using LightGBM, effectively classifies dermoscopic images into cancerous and non-cancerous lesions (Stage 1) and further categorizes non-cancerous lesions into specific benign diseases (Stage 2).
LightGBM outperformed other tested machine learning models (Naïve Bayes, SVM, Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, CatBoost) in terms of accuracy, recall, and F1-score.
The system achieved an accuracy of 81% in Stage 1 cancer detection and 85% in Stage 2 benign disease classification on the HAM10000 dataset.
The framework addresses class imbalance and data variability through image preprocessing, feature flattening, and SMOTE-based class balancing.

The model demonstrates high interpretability through feature importance analysis, confusion matrices, ROC curves, and probability distributions, offering a computationally lightweight alternative to deep learning methods.
The system shows potential for use in computer-aided diagnosis, teledermatology, and early cancer screening, particularly in clinical settings with limited resources.
While the model accurately identifies common conditions like nevus, it occasionally misclassifies complex or infrequent disorders such as vascular lesions and dermatofibroma.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset for training and evaluation, lacking external validation on independent datasets (e.g., ISIC or PAD) which limits generalizability assessment.

The framework uses image flattening to convert dermoscopic images into high-dimensional feature vectors for tree-based classifiers (LightGBM), which discards spatial information compared to deep learning approaches like CNNs.

Performance on Stage 2 benign classification shows occasional misclassifications for complex or infrequent disorders such as vascular lesions and dermatofibroma, indicating potential bias towards dominant classes like nevus.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance comparisons are based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics alone.
The paper does not report specific hyperparameters, training schedules, or exact dataset split ratios (training/validation/testing counts), limiting reproducibility.
The model addresses class imbalance using SMOTE, but the paper does not detail the specific parameters used for synthetic sample generation or potential risks of overfitting due to synthetic data.
The study is published as a conference paper in 2026, which may have shorter review cycles and less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.

While claimed to be computationally lightweight, the paper does not provide quantitative comparisons of inference time or memory usage against the deep learning models mentioned in the literature review.
